questions.question: Constructed as the grand entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair, this towering iron lattice structure on the Champ de Mars has become the definitive architectural symbol of Paris, France.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Anthony DELANOIX on Unsplash

questions.question: Perched on a narrow mountain ridge 7,970 feet above sea level, this astonishing 15th-century Incan citadel remained largely hidden from the outside world until its rediscovery by Hiram Bingham in 1911.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Eddie Kiszka on Unsplash

questions.question: Commissioned in 1632 by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan to house the tomb of his favorite wife, Mumtaz Mahal, this ivory-white marble mausoleum sits on the southern bank of the Yamuna River in Agra, India.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Annie Spratt on Unsplash

questions.question: Constructed under the Flavian dynasty between 72 AD and 80 AD, this massive freestanding oval amphitheater in the center of Rome once held up to 80,000 spectators for gladiatorial combats.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by David Köhler on Unsplash

questions.question: Designed by the visionary Catalan architect Antoni Gaudí, this breathtaking, organic-styled basilica in Barcelona has been under continuous construction since 1882.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Colin + Meg on Unsplash

questions.question: Famous for its rock-cut architecture and innovative water conduit system, this ancient Nabataean caravan city in Jordan is entered through a narrow sandstone gorge known as the Siq.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Brian Kairuz on Unsplash

questions.question: Originally constructed in the early 12th century as a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu before transforming into a Buddhist site, this massive complex in Cambodia is the largest religious monument in the world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by allPhoto Bangkok on Unsplash

questions.question: Designed by Frédéric-Auguste Bartholdi and dedicated in 1886, this colossal copper sculpture on Liberty Island was a gift from the people of France to the United States.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Fernando Strabuli on Unsplash

questions.question: Constructed out of reinforced concrete and soapstone between 1922 and 1931, this 98-foot-tall Art Deco statue of Jesus Christ stands atop the summit of Corcovado Mountain overlooking Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by gustavo nacht on Unsplash

questions.question: This sprawling hilltop palace and fortress complex in Granada, Spain, stands as one of the most famous surviving examples of Islamic architecture from the Nasrid dynasty.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jorge Fernández Salas on Unsplash

questions.question: Dominated by the massive step-pyramid known as El Castillo, this large pre-Columbian city built by the Maya civilization sits in the Mexican state of Yucatán.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Christina Abken on Unsplash

questions.question: Engineered by Joseph Strauss and opened in 1937, this iconic suspension bridge painted in a signature 'International Orange' hue spans the one-mile-wide strait connecting San Francisco to Marin County.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Maarten van den Heuvel on Unsplash

questions.question: Located on Salisbury Plain in Wiltshire, England, this prehistoric monument consists of an outer ring of vertical sarsen standing stones topped by horizontal lintels, erected around 2500 BC.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by K. Mitch Hodge on Unsplash

questions.question: Piercing the sky at a staggering 2,717 feet, this neo-Futurist mega-tall skyscraper in Dubai, United Arab Emirates, has reigned as the world's tallest building since its completion in 2010.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Nick Fewings on Unsplash

questions.question: Designed by Danish architect Jørn Utzon and formally opened in 1973, this Expressionist masterpiece on Bennelong Point features a series of distinctive gleaming white concrete 'shells' resembling sails.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Photoholgic on Unsplash

questions.question: Carved directly out of a single ridge of limestone on the west bank of the Nile, this colossal monolith depicts a mythical creature with the body of a lion and the head of an Egyptian pharaoh.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Hongbin on Unsplash

questions.question: Commissioned by the reclusive 'Mad' King Ludwig II of Bavaria in 1869 as a personal refuge, this Romanesque Revival cliffside palace served as the direct inspiration for Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Rachel Davis on Unsplash

questions.question: This ancient rocky citadel rises high above the Greek capital and contains the architectural remains of several historic structures of great universal significance, most notably the Parthenon.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Spencer Davis on Unsplash

questions.question: Sculpted by Gutzon Borglum into the granite face of the Black Hills in South Dakota, this massive national memorial features the 60-foot-tall faces of George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt, and Abraham Lincoln.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Stephen Walker on Unsplash

questions.question: Built in 537 AD as the patriarchal cathedral of the Byzantine Empire's capital, this architectural marvel in Istanbul is world-renowned for its massive, revolutionary pendentive dome.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Nabil Adham on Unsplash

questions.question: Commissioned by Ivan the Terrible in 1555 to commemorate the capture of Kazan, this vivid, geometric cathedral on Moscow's Red Square is famous for its uniquely shaped, brightly painted onion domes.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jaunt and Joy on Unsplash

questions.question: Commissioned by King Frederick William II to represent peace, this 18th-century neoclassical triumphal arch in Berlin stood stranded inside the exclusion zone of the Berlin Wall during the Cold War.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Florian Wehde on Unsplash

questions.question: Dating back to the 9th century during the Sailendra Dynasty, this massive geometric step-pyramid on the island of Java, Indonesia, stands as the world's largest Buddhist temple.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Mario La Pergola on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in southwestern Colorado, this rugged National Park protects over 600 ancient Ancestral Puebloan cliff dwellings, including the massive 150-room Cliff Palace built under overhanging rock ledges.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Wallace Bentt on Unsplash

questions.question: Rising dramatically over the Lhasa Valley from the side of Marpo Ri (Red Mountain), this massive, fortress-like sanctuary served as the winter residence of the Dalai Lamas until the 1959 Tibetan uprising.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Xuyu Chi on Unsplash

questions.question: Serving as the imperial palace for twenty-four emperors across the Ming and Qing dynasties, this massive, walled palace complex in the center of Beijing contains 980 surviving buildings across 180 acres.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by __ drz __ on Unsplash

questions.question: Designed principally by Donato Bramante, Michelangelo, Carlo Maderno, and Gian Lorenzo Bernini, this Renaissance masterpiece in Vatican City stands as the largest church building in the world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Fabio Fistarol on Unsplash

questions.question: Construction began in 1173 on this freestanding bell tower in Tuscany, Italy, which famously began to sink into its soft alluvial subsoil before the third of its eight stories was even completed.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Heidi Kaden on Unsplash

questions.question: Originally built as a humble hunting lodge, this sprawling, opulent chateau was transformed by King Louis XIV into the seat of absolute political power and courtly life in France.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Mathias Reding on Unsplash

questions.question: Carved by the Rapa Nui people between 1250 and 1500 AD out of compressed volcanic ash, these hundreds of monolithic human figures with oversized heads guard a remote Polynesian island.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Hal Cooks on Unsplash

questions.question: Piercing the skyline at 1,815 feet, this concrete communications and observation tower in downtown Toronto, Canada, reigned as the world's tallest freestanding structure from 1975 until 2007.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by adriana macias on Unsplash

questions.question: Home to the historic Djinguereber Mosque built from mud bricks and wood in 1327, this ancient trading hub in Mali flourished as a legendary center of Islamic scholarship under the Mali Empire.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Laurent Gence on Unsplash

questions.question: Opened in 1914 after a grueling, disease-plagued construction effort by France and the United States, this artificial 51-mile waterway utilizes a system of stepped locks to bisect the Isthmus of Panama.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Alex Pagliuca on Unsplash

questions.question: Housed inside a massive 12th-century fortress turned royal palace along the Seine River, this institution features a controversial 1989 glass pyramid entrance designed by I.M. Pei.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Michael Fousert on Unsplash

questions.question: Rising to a symmetrical, snow-capped cone of 12,389 feet, this active stratovolcano southwest of Tokyo stands as an immortal cultural and spiritual symbol of Japan.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Clay Banks on Unsplash

questions.question: This stunning tidal island and medieval Benedictine abbey off the coast of Normandy, France, is completely cut off from the mainland at high tide, reachable only via a winding causeway.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Customerly on Unsplash

questions.question: Completed in a blistering 13 months during the Great Depression, this 102-story Art Deco skyscraper in Midtown Manhattan held the title of the world's tallest building for nearly 40 years.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Kit Suman on Unsplash

questions.question: Located on a high plateau in central Turkey, this volcanic landscape is famous for its towering 'fairy chimney' rock formations, ancient underground cities, and skies filled with hot air balloons.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Timur Garifov on Unsplash

questions.question: Discovered accidentally by local farmers digging a well in 1974, this massive collection of thousands of life-sized clay soldiers was buried in Xi'an to protect China's first emperor in the afterlife.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Aaron Greenwood on Unsplash

questions.question: Officially renamed Elizabeth Tower in 2012, this neo-Gothic clock tower at the north end of the Houses of Parliament in London is universally known by the nickname of its massive, 13.7-ton main bell.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Marcin Nowak on Unsplash

questions.question: Also known as the Wailing Wall, this ancient limestone retaining wall in the Old City of Jerusalem was originally constructed by Herod the Great to support the outer courtyard of the Second Temple.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by JR Ross on Unsplash

questions.question: Discovered by a teenager and his dog in 1940, this complex of caves in southwestern France contains exceptionally detailed, 17,000-year-old Paleolithic wall paintings, primarily depicting large animals.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Caveman via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Constructed between 1609 and 1616 during the rule of Ahmed I, this historic Istanbul mosque is famous for its cascading domes, six minarets, and interior walls hand-painted with over 20,000 blue Iznik tiles.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by David Rodrigo on Unsplash

questions.question: Running for roughly 9 miles along the Atlantic coast of County Clare, these breathtaking, vertical shale and sandstone sea cliffs plunge up to 702 feet into the ocean as Ireland's most visited natural landmark.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jarek Skowron on Unsplash

questions.question: Built as the futuristic centerpiece of the 1962 Century 21 Exposition World's Fair, this 605-foot-tall tower features a flying-saucer-shaped observation deck dominating the skyline of Seattle, Washington.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Andrea Leopardi on Unsplash

questions.question: Dominated by a massive, 660-foot-tall column of sheer rock, this ancient fortress complex in Sri Lanka features a gateway carved into the shape of an enormous lion, complete with massive stone paws.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Dylan Shaw on Unsplash

questions.question: Commissioned by Napoleon in 1806 following his victory at Austerlitz, this monumental neoclassical arch stands proudly at the center of the Place Charles de Gaulle at the western end of the Champs-Élysées.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by ian kelsall on Unsplash

questions.question: Comprising a vast mix of decayed temples, chapels, and pylons, this massive open-air complex near Luxor, Egypt, is dominated by the awe-inspiring Great Hypostyle Hall, which contains 134 colossal sandstone columns.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Mo Gabrail on Unsplash

questions.question: Spelling out its name in 45-foot-tall white capital letters across Mount Lee in the Santa Monica Mountains, this landmark was originally erected in 1923 as a temporary advertisement for a local real estate development.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by De'Andre Bush on Unsplash

questions.question: Located at 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue in Washington, D.C., this iconic building serves as the official residence and workplace of the President of the United States.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by David Everett Strickler on Unsplash

questions.question: Visible from many aerial and satellite views, this ancient stone-and-brick fortification stretches thousands of miles across the northern borders of China to protect against historic invasions.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Craig Nagy (Frayed), Vancouver, Canada via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Carved out over millions of years by the Colorado River in Arizona, this immense, colorful, and deep natural gorge stands as one of the world's most famous natural wonders.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Luca Galuzzi (Lucag) via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Straddling the border between New York state and Ontario, Canada, this massive and thunderous group of three waterfalls is a world-famous destination for sightseers and boat tours.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Quistnix via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Nicknamed 'The Crossroads of the World', this brightly illuminated commercial intersection in Midtown Manhattan, New York City, is famous for its massive digital billboards and annual New Year's Eve ball drop.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Terabass via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Built as massive stone tombs for ancient Egyptian pharaohs like Khufu, these three towering structures have stood on a desert plateau outside Cairo for over 4,500 years.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Ricardo Liberato via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Spanning 843 acres in the middle of Manhattan, this massive, world-famous urban park features sprawling lawns, artificial lakes, walking paths, and a famous zoo.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Jet Lowe via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Located in the Himalayas on the border between Nepal and China, this towering, snow-capped peak holds the title of the tallest mountain above sea level on Earth.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Photo by Pavel Novak via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Featuring 775 rooms and the famous 'Changing of the Guard' ceremony, this massive London estate serves as the official administrative headquarters of the British monarch.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Diliff via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Standing on the South Bank of the River Thames, this massive, 443-foot-tall giant observation wheel offers tourists sweeping, panoramic views of London's skyline.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: User:Diliff via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: This famous four-mile stretch of South Las Vegas Boulevard in Nevada is world-renowned for its concentration of massive resort hotels, bright neon lights, and sprawling casinos.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Matthew Field, http://www.photography.mattfield.com via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: This active stratovolcano with a perfectly symmetrical, snow-capped cone stands as the tallest mountain in Japan and a legendary symbol of the country.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Clay Banks on Unsplash

questions.question: Mainly located in Wyoming, this legendary wilderness area became the world's very first National Park in 1872 and is famous for its wildlife and the 'Old Faithful' geyser.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Nicolasintravel on Unsplash

questions.question: This famous freestanding bell tower in Italy is known worldwide because its weak foundations caused it to noticeably tilt to one side shortly after construction began.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Heidi Kaden on Unsplash

questions.question: This colossal, 98-foot-tall Art Deco statue of Jesus Christ stands with his arms wide open atop Corcovado Mountain, looking down over the city of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by gustavo nacht on Unsplash

questions.question: Painted in a bright 'International Orange' color to stand out in the fog, this massive suspension bridge spans the opening of San Francisco Bay in California.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Maarten van den Heuvel on Unsplash

questions.question: This giant stone oval amphitheater in the center of Rome, Italy, was built by the ancient Roman Empire to hold massive gladiatorial games and public spectacles.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by David Köhler on Unsplash

questions.question: This iconic, white marble palace-like monument in Agra, India, was built by a grieving Mughal emperor in the 1600s to serve as a stunning tomb for his favorite wife.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Annie Spratt on Unsplash

questions.question: Though the name technically refers only to the massive 13-ton bell inside, this iconic, historic clock tower at the Houses of Parliament is a universal symbol of London.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Marcin Nowak on Unsplash

questions.question: Spelled out in massive 45-foot-tall white letters across Mount Lee in California, this landmark is the ultimate visual symbol of the American film industry.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by De'Andre Bush on Unsplash

questions.question: Located on the harbor in Australia's largest city, this iconic performing arts building is famous for its unique roof design, which looks like giant white sails or shells.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Caleb on Unsplash

questions.question: This towering, 102-story Art Deco skyscraper in Midtown Manhattan is famous for its open-air observation deck and for being climbed by King Kong in classic cinema.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Kit Suman on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in a grassy field in Wiltshire, England, this famous prehistoric mystery consists of a circular ring of massive standing stones erected thousands of years ago.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by K. Mitch Hodge on Unsplash

questions.question: This famous, narrow waterway connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Mediterranean Sea, separating Spain from Morocco.
Located on an island in the Seine River, this iconic, medieval Catholic cathedral in Paris is famous for its Gothic architecture, gargoyles, and flying buttresses.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Hannah Reding on Unsplash

questions.question: Situated on a rocky island in San Francisco Bay, this notorious former federal prison once held famous criminals like Al Capone.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Photographed by and copyright of (c) David Corby (User:Miskatonic, uploader) 2006. Cropped by Dr. Blofeld via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: This famous artificial waterway cuts through Central America, allowing ships to pass directly between the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Angel Silva on Unsplash

questions.question: As the world's largest art museum, this former Parisian palace features a famous glass pyramid entrance and houses the Mona Lisa.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Michael Fousert on Unsplash

questions.question: Bordered by Jordan and Israel, this famous saltwater lake sits at the lowest elevation on land and is so salty that swimmers float effortlessly.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: David Shankbone via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Sculpted into the granite face of the Black Hills in South Dakota, this massive monument features the 60-foot heads of four American presidents.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Stephen Walker on Unsplash

questions.question: Often confused with London Bridge, this iconic combined bascule and suspension bridge crosses the River Thames next to the Tower of London.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Diliff via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Hidden high in the Andes Mountains of Peru, this world-famous 15th-century Incan citadel is often called the 'Lost City of the Incas'.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Eddie Kiszka on Unsplash

questions.question: Though mostly demolished in 1989, preserved concrete stretches of this historical barrier still stand as a major landmark in Germany's capital city.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Noir via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Located on Mexico's Yucatán Peninsula, this ancient Mayan city is dominated by a massive, world-famous step-pyramid known as El Castillo.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Christina Abken on Unsplash

questions.question: This massive, incredibly opulent palace complex outside of Paris was built by the 'Sun King' Louis XIV and features the famous Hall of Mirrors.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Mathias Reding on Unsplash

questions.question: Standing tall near Tokyo, this active stratovolcano is Japan's highest peak and is instantly recognizable by its symmetrical, snow-capped cone.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Clay Banks on Unsplash

questions.question: Surrounded entirely by the city of Rome, this tiny walled independent city-state serves as the spiritual headquarters of the Roman Catholic Church.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Diliff via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Running through the heart of Manhattan's Theater District, this famous New York street is globally synonymous with commercial theater and massive musical productions.
Located in the Scottish Highlands, this deep, murky freshwater lake is famous worldwide due to alleged sightings of a mythical aquatic monster.
Built for the 1962 World's Fair, this 605-foot-tall tower features a saucer-shaped observation deck that dominates the skyline of Seattle, Washington.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Andrea Leopardi on Unsplash

questions.question: Visible from space off the northeastern coast of Australia, this massive natural wonder is the largest coral reef system on Earth.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: NASA, by MISR via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Located inside Vatican City, this massive Renaissance-era church is one of the largest in the world and features a giant dome designed by Michelangelo.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Fabio Fistarol on Unsplash

questions.question: Located near Naples, Italy, this active volcano is famous for its catastrophic eruption in 79 AD that buried the ancient Roman city of Pompeii.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Pastorius via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Located in Lower Manhattan, this short, historic street is globally recognized as the definitive geometric and cultural hub of American financial markets.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Daniel Lloyd Blunk-Fernández on Unsplash

questions.question: Connecting Manhattan and Brooklyn over the East River, this beautiful historic suspension bridge features iconic stone arches and a popular pedestrian walkway.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Alexander Rotker on Unsplash

questions.question: Stretching across the entire northern third of the African continent, this massive, arid landscape holds the title of the largest hot desert in the world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Wolfgang Hasselmann on Unsplash

questions.question: This massive, rectangular urban park in San Francisco is even larger than New York's Central Park and stretches all the way to the Pacific Ocean.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jeffrey Eisen on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in Tanzania, this massive, snow-capped dormant volcano stands as the highest mountain in Africa and the tallest free-standing mountain in the world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Stephan Bechert on Unsplash

questions.question: This world-famous upscale shopping street in Beverly Hills, California, is legendary for its ultra-luxury designer boutiques and celebrity sightings.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Tjerk van der Heijden on Unsplash

questions.question: This famous neoclassical triumphal arch stands proudly at the center of a massive traffic roundabout at the western end of the Champs-Élysées in Paris.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by ian kelsall on Unsplash

questions.question: This world-famous, winding Italian waterway serves as the main aquatic 'street' for gondolas and water buses traveling through the city of Venice.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Tomasz Zielonka on Unsplash

questions.question: This historic Spanish mission and fortress complex in San Antonio, Texas, was the site of a legendary 1836 battle during the Texas Revolution.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Gabriel Tovar on Unsplash

questions.question: As the principal public square of Venice, Italy, this massive, historic plaza is tightly bordered by St. Mark's Basilica and flocks of pigeons.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Jorge Láscar from Australia via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Dedicated to the goddess Athena, the majestic ruins of this ancient white marble temple dominate the rocky cliffside of the Acropolis in Athens, Greece.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Spencer Davis on Unsplash

questions.question: Painted in striking white and international orange zones to comply with aviation laws, this landmark Eiffel-inspired lattice tower stands tall in Japan's capital.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Louie Martinez on Unsplash

questions.question: This massive, rectangular central plaza in the heart of Madrid, Spain, is completely surrounded by historic three-story residential buildings with arches.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Eduardo Rodriguez on Unsplash

questions.question: Serving as the massive headquarters for the U.S. Department of Defense in Virginia, what building is famous for its unique five-sided geometric design.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: David B. Gleason from Chicago, IL via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Constructed along the Atlantic coast of Florida, this sprawling historical launch site serves as the ultimate primary hub for American human spaceflight.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by SpaceX on Unsplash

questions.question: This historic, open central square in Moscow sits next to the Kremlin and features the brightly colored onion domes of St. Basil's Cathedral. What is it called?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Alexey Fedotov on Unsplash

questions.question: This iconic, active volcano on the island of Sicily stands as the tallest active volcano in Europe and is in a near-constant state of activity.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Asa Rodger on Unsplash

questions.question: This ancient limestone retaining wall in the Old City of Jerusalem is the holiest site where Jews are permitted to freely gather and pray.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by JR Ross on Unsplash

questions.question: Stretching across fifteen blocks of Hollywood Boulevard in California, this landmark sidewalk features thousands of five-pointed terrazzo and brass stars.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Meg von Haartman on Unsplash

questions.question: This spectacular, giant marble fountain in Rome is world-famous for the tourist tradition of throwing a coin over your shoulder to ensure a return trip.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Michele Bitetto on Unsplash

questions.question: Located inside the Apostolic Palace in Vatican City, this historic chapel is world-famous for its magnificent ceiling frescoes painted by Michelangelo.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Calvin Craig on Unsplash

questions.question: Spanning the southern tip of Florida, this massive, subtropical marshland wilderness is famous for its unique ecosystem and wild alligators.
This historic complex of commercial buildings in Midtown Manhattan is famous for its massive sunken plaza, ice rink, and annual giant Christmas tree.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Kaydn Ito on Unsplash

questions.question: What famous landmark is shown in this photo?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jovyn Chamb on Unsplash

questions.question: Name the ancient structure pictured here.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Davi Pimentel on Unsplash

questions.question: What ancient citadel is pictured here?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Willian Justen de Vasconcellos on Unsplash

questions.question: What famous structure is shown in this photo?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Max van den Oetelaar on Unsplash

questions.question: What famous clock tower is pictured here?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jurica Koletić on Unsplash

questions.question: What famous performing arts venue is shown in this photo?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Dan Freeman on Unsplash

questions.question: What ancient city carved into rose-red cliffs is shown here?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Torsten Dederichs on Unsplash

questions.question: This stunning flower-shaped Bahá'í House of Worship in New Delhi, India, is one of the most visited buildings in the world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Muhammad Mahdi Karim via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Straddling the border of Zambia and Zimbabwe, this waterfall on the Zambezi River is the largest in the world by total area of falling water.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Someone35 via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: At 9,573 feet, this is the highest mountain in Greece and, in ancient Greek religion, the home of the twelve Olympian gods.
This ancient Roman city in southern Italy was catastrophically buried under volcanic ash and pumice following the eruption of a nearby volcano in 79 AD, preserving it nearly intact.
Home to the Pyramid of the Sun and the Pyramid of the Moon, this massive pre-Columbian city northeast of Mexico City was once one of the largest cities in the ancient world.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Diego Delso via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Straddling the border of France and Italy at 15,774 feet, this is the highest peak in the Alps and in all of Western Europe.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: Tinelot Wittermans via Wikimedia Commons

questions.question: Opened in 1869 and connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea, this 120-mile Egyptian waterway dramatically shortened shipping routes between Europe and Asia.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by samuel hanna on Unsplash

questions.question: This volcanic archipelago in the Pacific Ocean, belonging to Ecuador, was visited by Charles Darwin in 1835 and inspired his theory of natural selection through its unique wildlife.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Heidi Bruce on Unsplash

questions.question: Though slightly shorter than the Nile, this South American river carries more water than any other river on Earth and flows through the world's largest rainforest.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Ivars Utināns on Unsplash

questions.question: Straddling the border of Peru and Bolivia at an elevation of 12,507 feet, this is the world's highest navigable lake and the largest lake in South America by surface area.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by WILLIAN REIS on Unsplash

questions.question: This famous geyser in Yellowstone National Park earned its name from its consistent eruption schedule, shooting boiling water up to 184 feet in the air roughly every 60 to 110 minutes.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Emily Campbell on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in southern Utah, this national park is known for its hoodoos — tall, thin spires of rock formed by erosion — and for having the world's largest concentration of these unique geological formations.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Tim Golder on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in eastern Utah, this national park contains over 2,000 natural sandstone arches, including the famous Delicate Arch which appears on Utah's license plate.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Ken Cheung on Unsplash

questions.question: This national park in southwestern Utah is known for its steep red cliffs, The Narrows slot canyon, and Angels Landing — a trail that requires navigating chains bolted into sheer rock.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jamie Hagan on Unsplash

questions.question: Formed by the collapse of a volcano around 5,700 years ago, this lake in Oregon is the deepest lake in the United States at 1,943 feet and is renowned for its startling deep blue color.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Jeff Hopper on Unsplash

questions.question: Spanning the border of Argentina and Brazil, this system of 275 individual waterfalls is wider than Niagara Falls and Victoria Falls combined, and is set within a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Gábor Sz. on Unsplash

questions.question: Located on the northeastern coast of Northern Ireland, this area of about 40,000 interlocking basalt columns — most of them hexagonal — was formed by an ancient volcanic eruption.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Sean Kuriyan on Unsplash

questions.question: This stretch of coastline along the southern edge of Italy's Sorrentine Peninsula is famous for its dramatic cliffs, pastel-colored towns perched above the sea, and UNESCO World Heritage status.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Tom Podmore on Unsplash

questions.question: These iconic chalk cliffs on the English coast near the Strait of Dover reach heights of up to 350 feet and have long served as a symbol of Britain due to their visibility from France.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Heather Wang on Unsplash

questions.question: This string of five colorful cliff-hanging villages along a rugged stretch of the Italian Riviera coastline — connected by hiking trails and a railway — is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Mike L on Unsplash

questions.question: These long, narrow inlets carved by glaciers along the west coast of Norway — including the famous Geirangerfjord and Nærøyfjord — are listed as UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by admiratio on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in northeastern Vietnam, this UNESCO World Heritage site is famous for its emerald waters and thousands of towering limestone islands topped with rainforest.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Marina Lobato on Unsplash

questions.question: Located in Siberia, Russia, this is the world's deepest lake at 5,387 feet and holds approximately 20% of the world's unfrozen surface fresh water.
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Daria Gordova on Unsplash

questions.question: Located off the coast of Belize, this giant marine sinkhole — roughly 1,000 feet wide and 400 feet deep — is one of the world's top scuba diving destinations.
questions.imageCredit: Photo: U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) via Wikimedia Commons
